inference_unit.notations: - $Hg-1201$: $HgBa_{2}CuO_{4+\delta}$ - $Tl-1201$: $TlBa_{2}CuO_{5}$ - $Hg-1212$: $HgBa_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{6+x}$ - $Hg-1223$: $HgBa_{2}Ca_{2}Cu_{3}O_{8+x}$ - $T_{c}$: superconducting transition temperature - $\rho$: average mass density - $n$: number of $CuO_{2}$ layers per unit cell

premises.conjecture: The structural similarity between the mercury-based cuprate $HgBa_{2}CuO_{4+\delta}$ (Hg-1201) and the thallium-based cuprate $TlBa_{2}CuO_{5}$ (Tl-1201) suggests that a homologous series of compounds exists in the Hg-Ba-Ca-Cu-O system with the general chemical composition $HgBa_{2}Ca_{n-1}Cu_{n}O_{2n+2+\delta}$, where $n$ is an integer representing the number of $CuO_{2}$ layers per unit cell. [1]

conjecture.title: Correlation between Tc and CuO layers

premises.conjecture: In the Hg-Ba-Ca-Cu-O system, the superconducting transition temperature $T_{c}$ is expected to increase as the number of $Cu$-$O$ planes per unit cell increases, consistent with trends observed in analogous thallium-based and bismuth-based copper oxide superconducting systems. [5]

The specific superconducting phase $HgBa_{2}Ca_{2}Cu_{3}O_{8+x}$ (Hg-1223), which contains three $CuO_{2}$ layers per unit cell, is the phase responsible for the superconductivity observed at approximately $133$ K in Hg-Ba-Ca-Cu-O samples.
premises.assumption: For the purpose of estimating the superconducting volume fraction from magnetic screening (zero-field cooling susceptibility) data in Hg-Ba-Ca-Cu-O composite samples, the average mass density of the material is assumed to be $\rho \approx 6~g~cm^{-3}$.

reasoning.step: The authors prepared samples by mixing a precursor material $Ba_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{5}$ (obtained from nitrates sintered at $900^{\circ}C$ in $O_{2}$) with powdered $HgO$. The mixture was pressed into pellets, sealed in evacuated quartz tubes, and heated at $800^{\circ}C$ for 5 hours.
The authors noted that upon opening the steel containers, the quartz tubes were found to be broken, implying an uncontrolled atmosphere during the cooling or late heating stage.
The authors characterized the samples in their "as-sintered" state (before any post-synthesis annealing) and observed a maximum superconducting transition temperature ($T_{c}$) of only $\sim 117$ K.
A subset of the synthesized pellets was subjected to a specific post-synthesis annealing process: heating for 5 hours at $300^{\circ}C$ in flowing oxygen.
Resistivity measurements on the oxygen-annealed samples showed a sharp drop in resistivity starting at a maximum temperature of $T \approx 132.5$ K, reaching zero resistance at 95 K.

step.title: Magnetic Susceptibility Measurement of Annealed Samples

reasoning.step: Magnetic susceptibility measurements (Zero-Field Cooling and Field Cooling) on the oxygen-annealed samples revealed an onset of diamagnetism at $T_{c} \approx 133.5$ K.

Using the assumed density [@assumption-4], the authors calculated that the zero-field cooling susceptibility corresponded to $\sim 100\%$ screening, and field-cooling susceptibility reached $\sim 10\%$ (and $\sim 60\%$ of its low-T value at 125 K), confirming that the phase with $T_{c} \approx 133.5$ K is a dominant bulk phase.
The authors compared the transition temperatures obtained from the two processing conditions: $\sim 133$ K for the oxygen-annealed samples versus $\sim 117$ K for the as-sintered samples.

Based on the significant increase in $T_{c}$ from $\sim 117$ K to $\sim 133$ K, the authors deduced that the final oxygen treatment at $300^{\circ}C$ was effective and necessary to achieve the higher transition temperature.

inference_unit.conclusion: Post-synthesis annealing in flowing oxygen at $300^{\circ}C$ is required to maximize the transition temperature in the Hg-Ba-Ca-Cu-O system; as-sintered samples exhibit a significantly lower maximum $T_{c}$ of $\sim 117$ K compared to the $\sim 133$ K achieved after annealing.
